_meta.purpose: Platform notes for the side-income threshold tool (task 5) — Phase 1 skeleton. Each platform: what it reports on a 1099-K, and any platform-specific quirk that changes how the number maps to taxable income. The universal truth the tool leads with: a 1099-K reports GROSS payments (before fees, refunds, and shipping you collected), not profit — reconciling gross to net is most of the return.
_meta.phase: 1 — data, pending owner sign-off. Platform-specific quirk claims marked 'sourced_pending_owner_review' need a primary source (the platform's own tax/help page) before shipping.
_meta.schema: code -> { name, category, reports, quirk, existing_page|null, source_url, verify_status }

etsy.name: Etsy
etsy.reports: Gross sales processed through Etsy Payments, before Etsy fees. Sales tax Etsy collects and remits is generally excluded.
etsy.quirk: Fees, shipping labels, and materials are deductible against the gross — the 1099-K number is not your profit.

ebay.name: eBay
ebay.reports: Gross payments including shipping collected, before eBay fees.
ebay.quirk: Reselling personal items at a LOSS is not taxable income, but a 1099-K may still report the gross — the loss must be reconciled on the return (personal-item losses are not deductible, but they aren't taxable gains either).

depop.name: Depop
depop.quirk: Same personal-items-at-a-loss issue as eBay for closet clean-outs vs a reselling business — the hobby-vs-business line matters here.

poshmark.name: Poshmark
poshmark.reports: Gross sales. Poshmark collects and remits sales tax on the buyer's behalf, so sales tax is not part of the seller's reportable income.
poshmark.quirk: The sales-tax handling is the commonly-misunderstood point — confirm the exact 1099-K basis (before or after Poshmark's fee/commission) against Poshmark's tax page.

stockx.name: StockX
stockx.quirk: High-value flips are clearly a business; occasional personal resales sit closer to capital-asset treatment. Cost basis (what you paid) is the seller's to document.

twitch.name: Twitch
twitch.reports: Payouts may come on 1099-NEC (service income) and/or 1099-MISC (royalties) rather than 1099-K, depending on payout type — a key distinction for this platform.
twitch.quirk: Bits/subs vs royalty income can be reported on different forms; the SE-tax treatment follows the income type. Confirm current Twitch payout reporting.

youtube.name: YouTube
youtube.reports: AdSense payments typically on 1099-NEC, not 1099-K; US tax withholding may apply to some income types.
youtube.quirk: Not a 1099-K platform for most creators — the tool should route YouTube income to the SE-tax explanation, not the 1099-K threshold. Confirm current Google payout reporting.

uber_doordash.name: Uber / DoorDash / rideshare & delivery
uber_doordash.reports: Gig platforms report on 1099-NEC and/or 1099-K depending on income type (delivery fees vs incentives).
uber_doordash.quirk: The site already has dedicated gig pages that compute this — the side-income tool should POINT to them, not re-implement.

_owner_review_notes: Every 'reports' and 'quirk' claim needs the platform's own current tax/help page as the source before shipping — platform reporting behavior changes and varies by payout type.

Twitch and YouTube are often NOT 1099-K platforms (creator income lands on 1099-NEC/MISC). The tool's copy must not imply a 1099-K threshold governs them; route them to the SE-tax / income-tax explanation instead.
The hobby-vs-business distinction gets ONE strong shared explainer page every platform+state combination links to (per the task file), not a restatement per page.
